our_argument_summary: Section 3.5.2 argues that WIN-062's Tesla longitudinal wave claim is circular: the dome's disc diameter (40,030 km = 2 × 20,015 km) approximates the globe's equatorial circumference (40,075 km) because the disc_radius was fitted to WGS84 distances. Tesla's measured ~40,000 km path is the globe's circumference, not a disc diameter. Tesla's own patent (US 787412) describes wave propagation around a sphere, with spherical Earth drawings. The ~1.57c phase velocity is a known Earth-ionosphere waveguide effect on a spherical Earth, not aetheric longitudinal waves.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: This is a prose section, not a data section. It analyzes the provenance of the 40,030 km disc diameter used in WIN-062. The section cross-references Section 4.5.9 for evidence that test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py perform WGS84 least-squares fitting.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The section correctly identifies test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py as performing WGS84 fitting, consistent with Section 4.5.9's more detailed treatment. No code discrepancies found in this section.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: SEC-3.5.2 is a prose section, not a WIN. N/A for monitoring tags.

holes_found.description: WRONG DOI for Cummer 2000. The section cites Cummer 2000 with DOI 10.1049/ip-map:20030535, which points to IEE Proceedings - Microwaves, Antennas and Propagation (a 2003 IEE publication). Cummer's actual paper is: S. A. Cummer, 'Modeling electromagnetic propagation in the Earth-ionosphere waveguide,' IEEE Trans. Antennas Propag., Vol. 48, No. 9, pp. 1420-1429, 2000. The correct DOI is 10.1109/8.898776. This is a completely wrong journal — IEEE Trans. vs IEE Proceedings. A dome defender could use this to argue we don't check our own sources.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the Cummer DOI href from 'https://doi.org/10.1049/ip-map:20030535' to 'https://doi.org/10.1109/8.898776' in both generate-html.js (Section 3.5.2) and any occurrence in wins.json detail text. Verify the Wait 1966 DOI (10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) also resolves correctly — it returned a 404 on fetch, though the AGU DOI format is plausible for JGR Vol 71 Issue 4 p1171.

holes_found.description: The section doesn't explicitly address the dome's specific claim that the disc diameter is 'independently derived from H(r) geometry, Schumann, Polaris data.' We mention this claim once ('The dome claims this diameter is independently derived from H(r) geometry, Schumann, Polaris data') but never systematically debunk each of the three alleged independent derivations. A dome defender could say: 'You assert circularity but don't engage with our three derivation paths.' Section 4.5.9 apparently handles this, but a reader who only reads 3.5.2 would find the rebuttal incomplete.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief parenthetical or sentence after the 'independently derived' claim, e.g.: 'None of these derivations produces 20,015 km from first principles: the Schumann formula gives a cavity height (9,572 km), not a radius; Polaris observations constrain the pole position, not the ice-wall distance; and H(r) geometry gives the firmament profile, not the disc edge. The 20,015 km value appears as an input parameter in the curve-fitting scripts, not as an output of any derivation (see Section 4.5.9 for details).' This preempts the 'you didn't engage with our evidence' objection.

holes_found.description: The section states the dome's disc diameter 'equals the globe's circumference (40,075 km)' when 40,030 ≠ 40,075. The 45 km (0.11%) discrepancy is actually useful evidence — it suggests imprecise borrowing from globe parameters rather than independent derivation — but saying they 'equal' each other is technically imprecise. A dome defender could nitpick: '40,030 ≠ 40,075, so your circularity argument fails.' The WIN-062 curmudgeon review already flagged this at moderate severity.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'equals' to 'approximates' and note the 0.11% discrepancy explicitly. The discrepancy itself is evidence: independent derivation would be unlikely to land within 0.11% of the globe's circumference by coincidence, and the small residual is consistent with imprecise conversion from globe to disc parameters.

holes_found.description: The Wait 1966 DOI (10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) could not be verified — the URL returned a 404 error. The DOI format is consistent with AGU's old JGR coding scheme (JZ = Journal of Geophysical Research, 071 = vol 71, i004 = issue 4, p1171 = page 1171), but without confirming it resolves, a reader who clicks the link may get an error page. This undermines credibility on a section that is specifically about checking provenance.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify whether the DOI resolves at https://doi.org/10.1029/JZ071i004p01171. If it doesn't, the correct format for old AGU papers may have changed. Consider citing Wait's more accessible work (e.g., Wait 1962, 'Electromagnetic Waves in Stratified Media,' Pergamon Press) or the 1968 NBS Technical Note 300 (freely available at nvlpubs.nist.gov).

holes_found.description: The section doesn't explicitly connect to the duplicate-counting issue between WIN-001 and WIN-062. Both derive from the same Tesla patent and the same 0.08484s measurement. WIN-001 extracts frequency (11.78 Hz), WIN-062 extracts velocity (1.574c). Section 3.5.2 is the natural place to note this double-counting since it's analyzing WIN-062's provenance in detail, but the section focuses only on the circularity of the disc diameter argument. The WIN-062 curmudgeon review identified this as a stronger argument we should be making.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding a sentence: 'WIN-062 and WIN-001 both derive from the same Tesla measurement (0.08484s round-trip time in Patent US 787412) — one claims a frequency, the other a velocity. These are not independent predictions; counting them separately inflates the WIN total.' This reinforces the 'misleading' verdict.

holes_found.description: The section mentions 'superluminal phase/group velocity' in one place: 'a superluminal phase/group velocity in the Earth-ionosphere waveguide.' This is physically imprecise — the PHASE velocity exceeds c but the GROUP velocity does NOT (it's subluminal). The waveguide dispersion relation gives v_phase × v_group = c². Only the phase velocity is superluminal; the group velocity (which carries energy and information) is always ≤ c. Saying 'phase/group velocity' implies both are superluminal, which is wrong.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'superluminal phase/group velocity' to 'superluminal phase velocity' (removing 'group'). The final paragraph already correctly distinguishes this, but the earlier mention is imprecise.

stronger_arguments.description: The section could make a stronger geometric argument about what Tesla's measurement actually constrains. On a globe, Tesla's signal travels along the surface (great circle) — the path length is half the circumference (~20,037 km one-way). On a dome disc, the signal would travel in a straight line across the disc diameter (40,030 km one-way). These are geometrically incompatible: the globe path is along a curved surface, the disc path is a straight chord/diameter. Tesla's description ('over the earth's surface') unambiguously describes the former. The dome defender cannot simultaneously claim: (a) the signal path equals the disc diameter (40,030 km), and (b) Tesla's description of surface propagation is accurate. They must reject Tesla's own characterization of his experiment to use his data.

The section could note that the 1.574c vs 1.572c discrepancy (dome claims 1.574c, but Tesla's stated 471,240 km/s gives 1.572c) reveals the dome used 40,030 km in its calculation, not Tesla's value. Working backwards: 1.574 × 299,792.458 = 471,873 km/s, and 471,873 × 0.08484 = 40,034 km — very close to 2 × 20,015 = 40,030. The dome computed velocity from its own disc diameter, not from Tesla's stated velocity. This is further evidence the 'confirmation' is constructed, not discovered.

kernel_of_truth.description: The section itself correctly identifies a real kernel of truth in the dome's claim: Tesla's measurement is genuine physics, and the ~1.57c phase velocity is a real waveguide effect. The section also correctly notes that 40,030 km is internally consistent within the dome's own parameter set (2 × 20,015 km), avoiding the previous error of conflating it with the 28,210 km equatorial ring. The section's analytical approach — separating internal consistency from external provenance — is scientifically sound.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The internal consistency is expected (it's just 2 × radius) and doesn't constitute confirmation. The provenance argument remains fatal: the disc radius was fitted to WGS84 distances, which encode the globe's circumference. The 'confirmation' is tautological.

text_fingerprint.title: The Disc Diameter: 40,030 km and Its Provenance
text_fingerprint.figure_description: Tesla Patent 787412 full page with spherical Earth in Figure 1

deeper_analysis:
Section 3.5.2 is one of the stronger analytical sections in the review. It makes a clear, well-structured argument about circularity that flows logically: (1) here's what the dome claims, (2) here's what Tesla actually measured, (3) the numerical match is by construction because the dome's disc radius was fitted to globe distances. The Tesla patent figure is effective visual evidence.

**Strengths:**
- The circularity argument is sound and well-presented
- The Tesla patent image with spherical Earth is powerful visual evidence
- Correctly separates internal consistency (40,030 = 2 × 20,015) from external provenance
- The final paragraph on waveguide physics provides the standard physics explanation
- Good cross-reference to Section 4.5.9 for the WGS84 fitting evidence

**Weaknesses:**
1. The Cummer 2000 DOI is WRONG — points to an IEE Proceedings paper, not Cummer's IEEE Trans. Antennas Propag. paper. This is a major citation error that must be fixed.
2. The Wait 1966 DOI may not resolve (returned 404 on fetch). Needs verification.
3. The phrase 'superluminal phase/group velocity' is physically incorrect — only phase velocity is superluminal.
4. The dome's three claimed independent derivations (H(r), Schumann, Polaris) are mentioned but not individually debunked. A dome defender could say we're avoiding their evidence.
5. The '40,030 equals 40,075' language is imprecise — they differ by 45 km (0.11%).

**Dome Defender's Best Attack on This Section:**
A skilled dome defender would argue: (1) You cite two waveguide references, one with a wrong DOI — how carefully are you checking YOUR sources? (2) You claim our disc diameter was 'fitted to WGS84' but you don't show the fitting code or explain the fitting procedure — you just assert it and point to Section 4.5.9. (3) You admit Tesla's measurement gives ~40,000 km path length, and our model predicts ~40,030 km disc diameter — that's a 0.11% match between an 1905 measurement and our geometric model. You call it circular, but you haven't proven the causal chain: where's your evidence that 20,015 km was derived from 40,075/2 rather than from independent geometric analysis?

This rebuttal is partially addressed by the Section 4.5.9 cross-reference (which discusses the curve-fitting scripts), but Section 3.5.2 itself could be more explicit about the derivation chain. The strongest counter-counter is that 40,030 ≈ 40,075 is suspiciously close to the globe's circumference AND that no independent derivation of 20,015 km from dome geometry has ever been shown — the value just appears as a parameter.

**Relationship to code_analysis tags:**
The section doesn't directly reference code_analysis tag statistics, which is appropriate — this is a provenance/circularity argument, not a monitoring infrastructure critique. However, the section's claim that 'the disc radius of 20,015 km was itself calibrated to match globe-derived distances (as the curve-fitting scripts confirm)' should be verified against what Section 4.5.9 actually shows. If Section 4.5.9 demonstrates the fitting but not specifically that 20,015 km was an output, the claim is slightly overstated.

citations_verified: US Patent 787412 — link to patents.google.com/patent/US787412A is valid and publicly accessible. The patent text quotes in our review ('over the earths surface', 'region diametrically opposite the pole') are consistent with the patent content as verified during WIN-062 review.
test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py — referenced as dome repository scripts performing WGS84 fitting. Confirmed by Section 4.5.9 prose at generate-html.js line 1424.
citations_failed: Cummer 2000 DOI (10.1049/ip-map:20030535) — WRONG. This DOI prefix (10.1049) belongs to IEE/IET, not IEEE. Cummer's paper was published in IEEE Trans. Antennas Propag. The correct DOI is 10.1109/8.898776. This DOI appears in both the section prose (generate-html.js line 1074) and in the WIN-062 detail text (wins.json). Both must be corrected.
citations_unchecked: Wait 1966 DOI (10.1029/JZ071i004p01171) — DOI format is consistent with AGU's old JGR coding (vol 71, issue 4, page 1171) but the URL returned a 404 error on fetch. May be a valid DOI that the resolver struggles with, or may need a different format. Could not verify paper title, author, or content directly. Wait was a prolific author on Earth-ionosphere waveguide theory in the 1960s, so the attribution is plausible.
